headline: A Single Misconfigured Route-Map Blackholed 340 Ferguson Branches for 6 Hours. The ISP Said It Wasn't Their Problem.
subheadline: The BGP session was Established. The prefixes were advertised. But traffic was walking into a void — and nobody could figure out why until they checked the outbound policy.

lesson_pages.title: 11:47 AM — 'Established' Doesn't Mean 'Working'
lesson_pages.body: It was a Tuesday in Q4 — peak season for Ferguson's commercial plumbing business. At 11:47 AM Eastern, the NOC started getting tickets from the Southeast region. POS systems couldn't reach the ERP backend. Voice calls were dropping. Branch managers were calling their district IT contacts directly, which is never a good sign. The network engineer on duty pulled up the BGP summary on the primary edge router. The session with the ISP showed 'Established' — state 6, clean and green. Uptime showed weeks. But the received prefix count read zero. Zero prefixes from an ISP that should have been sending a full table or at least a default route. The session was alive but empty, like a phone call where nobody speaks. Here's the thing about BGP that separates senior engineers from junior ones: the session state is the least interesting part. BGP runs on TCP port 179, and once the three-way handshake completes and OPEN messages are exchanged, the session hits Established. But Established just means 'we agree to talk.' It doesn't mean useful routes are being exchanged, accepted, or installed in the RIB.

lesson_pages.title: eBGP vs iBGP — And Why Ferguson Runs Both
lesson_pages.body: Ferguson's network uses eBGP to peer with upstream ISPs and MPLS providers at the data centers and major hub sites. Those are external peers — different autonomous systems, typically one hop away, TTL of 1 unless you're doing multihop. The ISP advertises routes to Ferguson, Ferguson advertises its own prefixes back. Simple in theory. Inside Ferguson's own AS, iBGP carries routes between internal routers — between data centers, between hub sites that aggregate branch traffic. The critical difference: iBGP peers don't modify the AS_PATH when advertising routes to each other. That's by design — you don't want your own AS number appearing multiple times internally. But it creates the iBGP split-horizon rule: a route learned via iBGP cannot be advertised to another iBGP peer. This means you either need a full mesh of iBGP sessions (which doesn't scale past about 10 routers) or you need route reflectors. Ferguson uses route reflectors at the data centers. If a route reflector goes down, internal route distribution breaks even though every individual session might still show Established. This is the kind of failure mode that produces exactly the symptoms we saw: sessions up, routes missing.

lesson_pages.title: The Path Selection Tiebreaker — Weight, Local Pref, AS Path, MED
lesson_pages.body: The outage turned out to be a route-map applied to the wrong direction. A change the previous night — intended to adjust MED values on outbound advertisements to the secondary ISP — was accidentally applied inbound on the primary ISP peer. The route-map had a 'deny' clause that matched everything not explicitly permitted. Result: every inbound prefix was silently dropped. BGP didn't crash. It just had nothing to install. Understanding the path selection algorithm is mandatory because every troubleshooting session eventually comes back to it. The order, simplified: Highest Weight (Cisco proprietary, local to the router, default 0 for learned routes, 32768 for locally originated). Highest Local Preference (shared across iBGP, default 100, higher wins — this is how you prefer one ISP over another enterprise-wide). Shortest AS_PATH (fewer AS hops wins). Lowest MED (Multi-Exit Discriminator, a suggestion to your neighbor about which entry point to prefer — they can ignore it). After those, it goes to oldest route, lowest router-id, and shortest cluster-list. In practice at Ferguson, Weight and Local Preference resolve 90% of path decisions. MED only matters when you have multiple links to the same ISP and want to influence their inbound traffic. The fix that Tuesday took 4 minutes once diagnosed: remove the route-map from the inbound direction, do a soft clear ('clear ip bgp <neighbor> soft in'), and watch the prefixes flood back in.

lesson_pages.title: Troubleshooting Stuck Sessions and Missing Routes
lesson_pages.body: After that incident, the team built a BGP troubleshooting runbook. Step one: check 'show ip bgp summary' — not just for Established, but for the prefix count and state timer. A session stuck in Active state means the local router is trying to establish but the remote side isn't responding. Common causes: ACL blocking TCP 179, wrong neighbor IP, wrong ASN configured, MD5 authentication mismatch. A session in Idle state with a reset counter incrementing usually means the peer is actively refusing — check for maximum-prefix limits being hit, or the remote side having a 'neighbor shutdown' command. For missing routes specifically: 'show ip bgp neighbors <IP> received-routes' versus 'show ip bgp neighbors <IP> routes.' The difference is critical. Received-routes shows what the peer actually sent. Routes shows what survived your inbound policy. If received-routes has prefixes but routes doesn't, your inbound route-map or prefix-list is filtering them. If received-routes is empty, the peer isn't sending — call the ISP. At Ferguson, with 1,700 branches relying on centralized services, a BGP issue at a hub site doesn't produce one ticket. It produces hundreds. That Tuesday generated 340 branch-level incidents, all from a single misapplied route-map. The lesson was institutional: every BGP change now requires a peer review and a rollback timer.

headline: An MTU Mismatch on One Interface Partitioned an Entire Ferguson Distribution Center. The Adjacency Log Said 'ExStart' for 47 Minutes Before Anyone Noticed.
subheadline: OSPF was running. Hellos were flowing. But the database exchange never completed — and the SPF algorithm was routing traffic into a dead end.

lesson_pages.title: 3:22 PM — When ExStart Means 'Stop Everything'
lesson_pages.body: Ferguson's Dallas distribution center handles fulfillment for 200+ branches across Texas and Oklahoma. At 3:22 PM on a Thursday, a routine switch replacement in the server room triggered an OSPF adjacency failure that nobody saw coming. The old Catalyst was replaced with a new one. The port came up, OSPF hello packets started flowing, and the neighbor relationship reached the ExStart state — the point where the two routers negotiate who will be master and who will be slave in the database exchange. And then it just... stayed there. ExStart. For 47 minutes. No alarms fired because the interface was up/up. Hellos were being exchanged every 10 seconds. To the monitoring system, the link was healthy. But OSPF never moved past ExStart to Exchange, Loading, or Full. The routers couldn't agree on Database Description (DBD) packet sizes because the new switch had an MTU of 9216 (jumbo frames enabled by default in the new firmware) while the old infrastructure was running 1500. OSPF checks MTU during the DBD exchange. If they don't match, the adjacency stalls in ExStart or Exchange forever. It will never time out on its own. It will just sit there, stuck, while the network slowly loses convergence.

lesson_pages.title: Link-State Mechanics — How OSPF Actually Builds a Map
lesson_pages.body: OSPF is fundamentally different from distance-vector protocols like EIGRP. Every OSPF router builds a complete topological map of the network — the Link-State Database (LSDB). Routers don't share 'I can reach network X in 3 hops.' They share 'Here are all my directly connected links and their costs.' Every router in an area has an identical LSDB. Then each router independently runs the Dijkstra SPF algorithm against that database to compute the shortest path tree from its own perspective. This is why areas matter. If Ferguson ran all 1,700 branches in a single OSPF area, every link-state change — every branch router reboot, every WAN circuit flap — would trigger SPF recalculation on every router in the network simultaneously. That's a CPU event. At scale, it's a network-wide CPU event that causes convergence delays and can even crash underpowered branch routers. Ferguson's OSPF design uses Area 0 as the backbone connecting the data centers and major hub sites. Branch sites are in stub areas, which reduces the LSDB size at the branch by replacing external routes with a default route. The branch router doesn't need to know about every prefix in the enterprise — it just needs to know how to get to the hub, and the hub will sort it out from there.

lesson_pages.title: DR/BDR Elections and Why They Go Wrong
lesson_pages.body: On multi-access segments — like a VLAN in a distribution center with 8 routers — OSPF elects a Designated Router (DR) and a Backup Designated Router (BDR). Without this, every router would need a full adjacency with every other router: an O(n-squared) problem. Instead, all routers form full adjacency only with the DR and BDR, and the DR handles flooding LSAs to everyone. The election is based on OSPF priority (0-255, default 1) and then router-id as a tiebreaker. Here's what trips people up: the DR election is non-preemptive. If your intended DR (priority 255) reboots, the BDR becomes DR, and a new BDR is elected from the remaining routers. When the original DR comes back, it doesn't reclaim the role. It becomes DROther. This leads to situations where the least powerful router in a segment ends up as DR because it happened to boot first, and it stays DR until something forces a re-election. At Ferguson's DCs, every OSPF segment has explicit priority configuration — core switches get priority 200, distribution gets 100, everything else gets 0. Setting priority 0 means 'never become DR,' which is what you want for branch routers on a hub-and-spoke segment. If you see a branch router serving as DR and adjacencies are flapping, this is almost certainly the problem.

lesson_pages.title: Common Failures — The Checklist That Saves Hours
lesson_pages.body: After the Dallas MTU incident, the team cataloged the OSPF failures they'd seen at Ferguson over the previous two years. The top five: MTU mismatch (adjacency stuck in ExStart/Exchange — fix by matching MTU or using 'ip ospf mtu-ignore' as a temporary hack, never permanent). Hello/Dead timer mismatch (default is 10/40 on broadcast, 30/120 on NBMA — if one side is changed and the other isn't, the adjacency never forms because each side thinks the other is dead before the next hello arrives). Area mismatch (two routers on the same segment configured in different areas — adjacency will not form, and the log message is easy to miss among all the other noise). Authentication mismatch (MD5 key-id or password doesn't match — adjacency drops to Init and stays there). Duplicate router-ids (two routers with the same router-id in the same area cause LSA conflicts and unpredictable routing — this happens more often than you'd think when cloning VM routers or restoring configs from backup). Every single one of these produces the same customer-facing symptom: 'The network is slow' or 'I can't reach the server.' The difference between a 10-minute resolution and a 2-hour war room is knowing how to read 'show ip ospf neighbor,' 'show ip ospf interface,' and 'debug ip ospf adj' without panicking.

headline: Ferguson's Branch VPN Fabric Collapsed When a Single Hub Router Ran Out of NHRP Entries. 1,200 Spokes Lost Connectivity in Under 90 Seconds.
subheadline: The branches could still reach the internet. They just couldn't reach each other — or the data center. NHRP had silently hit its limit, and nobody had set a threshold alarm.

lesson_pages.title: 6:15 AM — The Day the Spokes Went Dark
lesson_pages.body: Ferguson connects its 1,700 branches back to the data centers using DMVPN — Dynamic Multipoint VPN. It's the workhorse of enterprise branch connectivity and has been for over a decade. At 6:15 AM on a Monday, branches in the Midwest region started losing connectivity to centralized applications. POS couldn't process returns that required ERP lookups. VoIP calls to the service desk wouldn't connect. But internet browsing worked fine — the local breakout was still functioning. The issue was the DMVPN hub router at the regional data center. It had been gradually accumulating NHRP (Next Hop Resolution Protocol) entries as branches registered and re-registered after weekend maintenance windows. The router's NHRP table hit a memory-constrained limit, and new registrations started failing silently. Spokes whose NHRP registration expired couldn't re-register. Their tunnel interface stayed up, the GRE encapsulation was fine, but without a valid NHRP entry at the hub, traffic from the hub couldn't find the path back to the spoke. It's like having a phone that can dial out but isn't listed in the directory — nobody can call you back.

lesson_pages.title: Phase 1, 2, and 3 — What Ferguson Actually Uses and Why
lesson_pages.body: DMVPN has three phases, and understanding them matters because they define how traffic flows. Phase 1 is pure hub-and-spoke: all traffic between branches passes through the hub. Spoke A wants to talk to Spoke B? The packet goes Spoke A to Hub to Spoke B. Simple, predictable, but the hub is a bottleneck and a single point of failure. This is what you'd use for a small deployment. Phase 2 enables spoke-to-spoke tunnels. Spoke A wants to reach Spoke B, so it sends an NHRP Resolution Request to the hub, gets back Spoke B's public IP, and builds a direct tunnel. Traffic flows directly between branches without hairpinning through the hub. The catch: Phase 2 requires that the spoke not summarize or change the next-hop of routes — which breaks most routing protocol configurations. Phase 3 solves this with NHRP shortcuts and redirects. The hub can send an NHRP Redirect message telling spokes to build direct tunnels, even when the routing table points to the hub as the next-hop. This is what Ferguson runs. Phase 3 with EIGRP or BGP as the overlay routing protocol. It gives you the scalability of hub-and-spoke routing with the efficiency of direct spoke-to-spoke data paths. But Phase 3 is also the most complex to troubleshoot because you're dealing with NHRP redirects, shortcut routes that override the routing table, and IPSec SAs that need to be negotiated on-demand between spokes that may have never talked before.

lesson_pages.title: IPSec Integration and Tunnel Failures
lesson_pages.body: Every DMVPN tunnel at Ferguson is encrypted with IPSec. The GRE tunnel provides the multipoint capability — multiple destinations on a single tunnel interface. IPSec provides confidentiality and integrity. They work together but fail independently, which makes troubleshooting interesting. The typical stack: a Tunnel interface configured with 'tunnel mode gre multipoint' and 'tunnel protection ipsec profile <name>.' The IPSec profile references a transform set (AES-256, SHA-256) and an IKEv2 policy. When a spoke registers with the hub, it also negotiates an IPSec SA. If IKE fails — pre-shared key mismatch, crypto proposal mismatch, NAT-traversal issues — the GRE tunnel might actually come up but traffic won't flow because IPSec drops it. The most common DMVPN tunnel failures at Ferguson branches: ISP-side NAT breaking IKE (solved by enabling NAT-T on UDP 4500). MTU issues where the GRE+IPSec overhead (overhead is roughly 60-80 bytes depending on the crypto suite) pushes packets over the path MTU and they get dropped — solved by setting 'ip mtu 1400' and 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' on the tunnel interface. Certificate expiration causing IKE to fail silently — this one bit Ferguson hard when a batch of 400 branch router certificates expired on the same day because they'd been provisioned in a single bulk operation three years earlier.

lesson_pages.title: Troubleshooting Like You've Done This Before
lesson_pages.body: The DMVPN troubleshooting sequence that the Ferguson network team uses daily: First, 'show dmvpn' — this gives you the NHRP peer table, the tunnel state (Up, NHRP, IKE), and the attrribute flags. If a spoke shows 'IKE' instead of 'Up,' the GRE tunnel is formed but IPSec hasn't completed. If it doesn't appear at all, NHRP registration hasn't happened. Second, 'show ip nhrp' — check if the spoke's NHRP entry exists on the hub. If it's missing, the spoke either can't reach the hub's NBMA (physical) address, or registration is being rejected. Third, 'show crypto ikev2 sa' — verify the IKE Security Association exists and is in the READY state. If it's in a partial state, check crypto proposals, pre-shared keys, and certificate validity. Fourth, 'show crypto ipsec sa' — verify the IPSec SA is encrypting and decrypting packets. If the encrypt counter increments but decrypt doesn't, traffic is going out but nothing is coming back — likely an asymmetric routing issue or the remote side's IPSec SA is stale. At Ferguson's scale, the single hardest DMVPN problem is 'one branch can't connect and we don't know why.' It's almost always one of four things: the ISP changed the branch's public IP and NHRP hasn't re-registered, a firewall rule on the ISP side is blocking UDP 500/4500, the branch router rebooted and the hub still has a stale NHRP entry, or someone fat-fingered the tunnel source interface during a config change.

headline: Ferguson's MPLS Provider Had a PE Router Failure. 87 Branches Went Down — and the Provider's SLA Said 4-Hour Response. It Took 11.
subheadline: The branch routers were healthy. The LAN was fine. But the label-switched path through the provider's cloud had vanished, and there was nothing Ferguson could do except call and escalate.

lesson_pages.title: 9:30 AM — 'It's Not Our Network, It's Theirs'
lesson_pages.body: Ferguson uses MPLS VPN service from a major carrier to connect roughly 500 of its larger branch locations — the ones with enough bandwidth needs to justify a private circuit over commodity internet with DMVPN. At 9:30 AM on a Wednesday, 87 of those branches in the Ohio Valley region simultaneously lost connectivity to the data center. The Ferguson NOC immediately started the triage. Branch routers were up. LAN switches were fine. The WAN interface on the CE (Customer Edge) routers showed up/up. BGP sessions between the CE and the provider's PE (Provider Edge) router were... down. Every single one in that region. The provider's PE router had crashed. This is the fundamental tension of MPLS: it provides excellent traffic isolation and QoS capabilities, but you're trusting someone else's infrastructure for your most critical connectivity. Ferguson's CE routers peer with the provider's PE routers using eBGP. What happens inside the provider's cloud — the P (Provider) routers, the label distribution, the traffic engineering — is completely opaque. When it breaks, you open a ticket and wait.

lesson_pages.title: CE, PE, P — Who Controls What
lesson_pages.body: Understanding the MPLS VPN architecture is essential because it defines what you can troubleshoot and what you can only escalate. The CE router is Ferguson's. It sits at the branch, runs the LAN, and peers with the provider's PE router using eBGP (typically). Ferguson's team has full control: they manage the routing policy, the BGP advertisements, the interface configuration. The PE router is the provider's edge device — the handoff point. It runs MP-BGP (Multiprotocol BGP with the VPNv4 address family) to carry Ferguson's routes across the provider backbone while keeping them isolated from other customers in separate VRFs. Ferguson never touches the PE config, but understanding how it works is critical for troubleshooting. The P routers are deep inside the provider's core. They don't know anything about Ferguson's routes. They only know about labels — MPLS labels that tell them where to forward packets. This is the beauty and the curse of MPLS: the core is simple and fast (label switching is more efficient than IP lookup), but the complexity is concentrated at the PE routers. When a PE goes down, every customer on that PE loses their VPN connectivity simultaneously. Ferguson's 87-branch outage was a 15-customer outage from the provider's perspective.

lesson_pages.title: MP-BGP and Route Targets — The VPN Glue
lesson_pages.body: Inside the provider's network, Ferguson's routes are carried using MP-BGP with the VPNv4 address family. Each route is tagged with a Route Distinguisher (RD) that makes it globally unique — even if Ferguson uses the same 10.0.0.0/8 space as another customer, the RDs keep them separate. Route Targets (RTs) are the import/export controls. The PE router exports Ferguson's routes with a specific RT (say, 65000:100). At the remote PE, routes are imported into Ferguson's VRF only if the import RT matches. This is how the provider builds the VPN topology: full mesh, hub-and-spoke, or any combination. From Ferguson's perspective, the key thing to understand about RTs is that they define reachability. If a new branch is provisioned and the provider forgets to configure the correct import/export RTs on the PE, that branch will be an island — the CE-PE BGP session will come up, routes will be advertised, but they'll never appear at any other PE in the VPN. The branch will look healthy from every local diagnostic but won't be able to reach anything. This has happened at Ferguson more than once during new site deployments, and the diagnosis is always the same: 'We can see the PE accepting our routes, but they're not showing up at the hub PE. Please check your RT configuration.' It takes 30 seconds to fix once the provider acknowledges it.

lesson_pages.title: What You Control vs What You Escalate
lesson_pages.body: After the Ohio Valley outage, Ferguson's network leadership built a clear escalation matrix for MPLS issues. Things you own and can fix: CE router configuration, BGP policy on your side, route advertisements and filtering, failover to backup DMVPN tunnels (which is why every MPLS branch also has an internet circuit for redundancy). Things you can verify but not fix: the CE-PE physical link (you can see your interface status and errors, but if the problem is on the provider's side of the demarc, you escalate), the BGP session to the PE (you can see your side, and 'show ip bgp neighbors' will tell you if the remote side is sending incorrect capabilities or resetting the session, but you can't log into the PE). Things that are completely opaque: anything past the PE. Label distribution, MPLS core routing, P router health, traffic engineering tunnels. If 'show ip bgp summary' shows the PE session as Established with correct prefix counts, and 'traceroute' through the MPLS cloud is working, your MPLS is healthy. If the session is down or prefixes are missing and you've verified your CE config is correct, it's a provider problem. Full stop. Document what you've checked, open the ticket with specifics ('BGP session to PE 192.168.1.1 in state Active since 09:30, our interface is up/up with zero errors, MD5 auth configured and unchanged'), and escalate. Specificity in the ticket saves hours because it prevents the provider's Tier 1 from running their script that tells you to reboot your router.

headline: A Ferguson Branch Manager Called the CIO Directly: 'Every Customer Call Sounds Like We're Underwater.' The WAN Link Was Only at 60% Utilization.
subheadline: Bandwidth wasn't the problem. A backup job was monopolizing the queue, and the voice packets were waiting in line behind 200MB database syncs.

lesson_pages.title: 2:00 PM — '60% Utilization' Is a Lie
lesson_pages.body: A Ferguson branch in Charlotte was averaging 60% utilization on its 100 Mbps WAN circuit. By every bandwidth monitoring metric, there was 40 Mbps of headroom. Plenty of room for the 6 concurrent VoIP calls the branch typically handled. But the MOS (Mean Opinion Score) for calls had dropped to 2.8 — barely intelligible. The branch manager, after three days of escalating through normal channels, called the CIO directly. This triggered a priority investigation. The answer was microbursts. That 60% was a 5-minute average. In reality, the ERP database sync that ran every 15 minutes was blasting 95 Mbps for 30-45 seconds at a time. During those bursts, the egress queue on the WAN interface filled up, and voice packets — which are tiny (roughly 200 bytes each for G.711) but extremely latency-sensitive — were sitting behind megabytes of database traffic waiting for their turn. VoIP needs less than 150ms one-way latency, less than 30ms jitter, and less than 1% packet loss to sound acceptable. A 45-second burst that adds 200ms of queuing delay destroys call quality even though the 5-minute average looks fine. This is why QoS exists: not to add bandwidth, but to guarantee that critical traffic gets served first when contention occurs.

lesson_pages.title: Classification, Marking, and the MQC Framework
lesson_pages.body: QoS at Ferguson uses Cisco's Modular QoS CLI (MQC) framework, which has three components. Class-maps define what traffic you care about. You match traffic using DSCP markings, ACLs, or NBAR (Network-Based Application Recognition) for deep packet inspection. For example: 'class-map match-all VOICE' with 'match dscp ef' identifies voice RTP traffic marked with Expedited Forwarding. Policy-maps define what you do with that traffic. Inside the policy-map, each class gets a treatment: 'priority percent 30' gives the VOICE class a strict priority queue (Low Latency Queue) with 30% of the link bandwidth guaranteed. 'bandwidth percent 40' gives another class a minimum bandwidth guarantee without strict priority. 'fair-queue' gives a default class fair treatment with WFWQ. Service-policy applies the policy-map to an interface in the correct direction: 'service-policy output WAN-EGRESS' on the WAN-facing interface. The most common mistake Ferguson engineers have made: applying the policy in the wrong direction. QoS shaping and queuing only work on egress (outbound). You can classify and mark on ingress, but you can't queue traffic that's arriving — it's already arrived. If your policy-map has queuing statements and you apply it inbound, it either does nothing or the router rejects it entirely.

lesson_pages.title: LLQ for Voice and Why Policing Isn't Shaping
lesson_pages.body: The Low Latency Queue (LLQ) is what makes VoIP work on congested WAN links. When you configure 'priority' in a policy-map class, that class gets a strict priority queue — its packets are always dequeued first, ahead of all other classes. But strict priority has a built-in policer. If you configure 'priority 3000' (3 Mbps), and the voice traffic exceeds 3 Mbps, the excess is dropped. This prevents a misbehaving voice flow from starving everything else. This brings up the critical distinction between policing and shaping. Policing drops traffic that exceeds the rate — immediately, with no buffering. It's typically used for ingress enforcement or LLQ rate-limiting. Shaping buffers traffic that exceeds the rate and sends it later when bandwidth is available. It smooths bursts instead of dropping them. Shaping adds latency (the buffering delay) but avoids loss. Policing adds loss but no latency. For voice: you want LLQ with a policer (excess voice traffic is dropped, which is acceptable because excess voice traffic usually means a misconfigured phone flooding the network). For data: you want shaping on the WAN interface to the committed rate so that TCP traffic gets buffered and retransmitted gracefully instead of being dropped, which causes TCP retransmissions, window collapse, and terrible throughput.

lesson_pages.title: Ferguson's Standard Branch QoS Policy
lesson_pages.body: After the Charlotte incident, Ferguson standardized the branch QoS policy across all 1,700 locations. The model allocates WAN bandwidth as follows: Voice (DSCP EF) gets strict priority at 18% — enough for 10 concurrent G.711 calls on a 20 Mbps link. Signaling and Call Control (DSCP CS3/AF31) gets 5% bandwidth guarantee — SIP and SCCP traffic that sets up and tears down calls. Critical Data (DSCP AF21) gets 30% — ERP transactions, POS authorization traffic, database replication. Default (DSCP 0) gets 25% — general browsing, email, everything else. Scavenger (DSCP CS1) gets 5% — software updates, non-critical backups. The remaining 17% is shared across classes during non-contention. Troubleshooting voice quality at Ferguson now follows a standard flow: check 'show policy-map interface <WAN>' to see if the LLQ is seeing drops (if voice packets are being policed/dropped, either the bandwidth allocation is too low or something is being misclassified into the voice queue). Check 'show ip nbar protocol-discovery' to verify that NBAR is correctly identifying the traffic. Check the jitter/latency from the IP SLA probes that run from every branch to the data center. Nine times out of ten, voice quality issues at a branch come down to either the ISP circuit being degraded (packet loss on the physical link, which no amount of QoS can fix) or a misclassification putting bulk traffic into the priority queue and triggering policing drops on legitimate voice.

headline: A vPC Peer-Link Failure at Ferguson's Primary Data Center Split the Switching Fabric in Half. Both Sides Thought They Were the Primary.
subheadline: The keep-alive was still up but the peer-link was down. The secondary switch didn't suspend its vPC member ports. Two active gateways, two HSRP primaries, one very bad day.

lesson_pages.title: 4:47 AM — The Split-Brain That Shouldn't Have Happened
lesson_pages.body: Ferguson's primary data center runs a pair of Cisco Nexus 9000 switches as the aggregation layer, connected via vPC (virtual Port Channel). Every downstream switch, server rack, and firewall dual-homes to both Nexus switches. It's a clean, well-understood design — until 4:47 AM on a Saturday when a fiber patch panel was bumped during a cabling project and both peer-link member interfaces went down simultaneously. The vPC peer-link carries data traffic between the two switches and synchronizes MAC tables, ARP entries, and IGMP state. When it goes down, the secondary switch is supposed to suspend all its vPC member ports to prevent a split-brain scenario. But the peer-keepalive link — a separate L3 path used as a heartbeat — was still up. And due to a misconfigured peer-keepalive destination (it was pointing to a management interface that was always reachable via an out-of-band path), the secondary switch received conflicting signals: the peer-link said 'partner is gone,' but the keepalive said 'partner is alive.' The result was a 14-minute window where both switches were acting as primary — both answering ARP requests, both serving as HSRP active, both forwarding traffic. MAC table inconsistencies caused intermittent connectivity for every server in the DC. The monitoring systems themselves were affected, so alerts were delayed.

lesson_pages.title: vPC Architecture — The Contract Between Two Switches
lesson_pages.body: vPC lets two physical Nexus switches appear as a single logical switch to downstream devices. A server with two NICs can run LACP across both switches and see a single port-channel. This eliminates Spanning Tree blocked ports and doubles available bandwidth. The architecture has three critical components: the vPC peer-link (a 10G or higher port-channel between the two switches that syncs control plane data and carries orphan traffic), the peer-keepalive link (a lightweight L3 heartbeat, typically over the management VRF, used only to detect if the peer switch is alive when the peer-link fails), and the vPC member ports (the actual port-channels going to downstream devices). The operational contract: when the peer-link fails but the keepalive is up, the secondary switch knows the primary is alive and suspends its vPC member ports to avoid dual-active. When both the peer-link AND keepalive fail, the secondary assumes the primary is dead and keeps its ports active (because someone has to forward traffic). This is why the keepalive path must be truly independent — a dedicated link or a management network that doesn't traverse the peer-link. Ferguson's mistake was routing the keepalive through a path that could never fail, which broke the failure detection logic.

lesson_pages.title: NX-OS vs IOS — A Different Operating System, A Different Mindset
lesson_pages.body: Engineers coming from IOS to NX-OS hit several surprises. First: features must be explicitly enabled. Want to run OSPF? 'feature ospf' first. Want LACP? 'feature lacp.' VPC? 'feature vpc.' If you try to configure something without enabling the feature, NX-OS will reject the command entirely. This is intentional — it keeps the control plane lightweight by only loading the processes you need. Second: the configuration hierarchy is different. NX-OS uses a more Linux-like structure with VDCs (Virtual Device Contexts, on older Nexus 7000s) and VRFs as first-class concepts. Interface naming follows the module/port convention (Ethernet1/1, not GigabitEthernet0/1). Third: 'show' commands are similar but not identical. 'show ip route' works but 'show ip bgp' becomes 'show bgp ipv4 unicast.' 'show run interface' works. 'show vpc' gives you the vPC-specific status that doesn't exist in IOS. Fourth: NX-OS has checkpoints and rollback built in. 'checkpoint <name>' saves the running config, and 'rollback running-config checkpoint <name>' reverts to it. This is enormously useful in the data center where a bad change can affect hundreds of servers. Ferguson's DC team uses checkpoints before every change — the Saturday incident was actually recoverable because someone had checkpointed the config during the cabling project, and rolling back the peer-keepalive configuration was a single command.

lesson_pages.title: Troubleshooting vPC — The Consistency Check
lesson_pages.body: After the split-brain incident, Ferguson's DC team built a vPC health verification checklist that runs before and after every maintenance window. The key commands: 'show vpc' — shows the overall vPC domain status, peer-link status, keepalive status, and the role (primary/secondary) of each switch. If this shows 'peer-link is down' and 'peer-keepalive is alive,' you have about 3 seconds before the secondary suspends ports (if configured correctly). 'show vpc consistency-parameters global' and 'show vpc consistency-parameters interface' — these are critical. vPC requires certain parameters to match between both switches: STP mode, VLAN membership on the peer-link, QoS policies, MTU settings, LACP mode. If there's a mismatch, vPC will either suspend the affected member port or, worse, bring up the vPC in an inconsistent state that fails unpredictably under load. A 'Type 1 consistency check' mismatch (STP, LACP, vPC domain config) will suspend the vPC. A 'Type 2 mismatch' (MTU, trunk allowed VLANs) will log a warning but won't suspend — which means it will silently cause problems. At Ferguson, every vPC change goes through a pre-check: stage the config on the secondary first, run 'show vpc consistency-parameters,' verify no mismatches, then apply to the primary. The order matters because the secondary is the one that suspends ports on a conflict — staging there first lets you catch mismatches before they're live.

headline: Ferguson Piloted SD-WAN at 12 Branches. Zero-Touch Provisioning Worked Perfectly — Until the Branch With Two ISPs and a Satellite Backup Made the Overlay Flap Every 90 Seconds.
subheadline: The Director showed green. Analytics showed green. But the traffic steering policy couldn't handle a WAN link with 700ms latency and 6% packet loss as a 'valid path.'

lesson_pages.title: The POC — What Ferguson Evaluated and Why
lesson_pages.body: Ferguson participated in a Versa SD-WAN proof of concept covering 12 branch locations across three states. The goal was evaluating whether SD-WAN could replace or augment the existing DMVPN fabric at smaller branches where MPLS was too expensive and DMVPN management overhead was high. Full transparency: this was a POC/pilot engagement, not a production deployment at scale. The team gained hands-on experience with the Versa architecture but did not own or operate it as production infrastructure across the 1,700-branch footprint. The Versa architecture has three control plane components: the Director (the centralized management and orchestration platform — think of it as the brain that pushes policy to all devices), Analytics (collects telemetry, flow data, and application performance metrics from every branch appliance), and the Controller (handles route distribution and secure connectivity between branches). At the branch, a Versa FlexVNF appliance replaces the traditional router. It builds encrypted overlay tunnels to hub sites and other branches over whatever underlay circuits are available — MPLS, broadband, LTE, satellite. The fundamental promise of SD-WAN is abstracting the underlay: the business policy says 'voice traffic must take the lowest-latency path' and the appliance figures out which physical circuit that is, in real time.

lesson_pages.title: ZTP and Overlay/Underlay Separation
lesson_pages.body: Zero-Touch Provisioning was the feature that most impressed Ferguson's team during the POC. A new branch appliance arrives, gets plugged into a broadband circuit with DHCP, phones home to the Director using a pre-loaded cloud gateway address, authenticates with a serial number and OTP, downloads its complete configuration, builds its overlay tunnels, and starts forwarding traffic. No console cable. No USB drive with a config file. No engineer flying to the branch. For an organization with 1,700 branches where router replacements happen weekly, ZTP changes the operational model completely. The overlay/underlay separation is the core architectural concept. The underlay is the physical connectivity — whatever ISP circuits exist at the branch. The overlay is the encrypted tunnel mesh that SD-WAN builds on top. Routing in the overlay is independent of the underlay. If a branch has two ISP circuits, the overlay sees two paths to the hub. If one circuit degrades, the overlay can shift traffic to the other within seconds, without any routing protocol convergence. This is fundamentally different from DMVPN, where the overlay routing (EIGRP or BGP running over the tunnel) has to converge, which takes 30-90 seconds depending on timer configuration. SD-WAN overlays can shift in under 5 seconds because the decision is policy-based, not protocol-based.

lesson_pages.title: The Satellite Link Problem — When Steering Policy Meets Physics
lesson_pages.body: The POC hit its biggest challenge at a rural Oklahoma branch that had a 25 Mbps broadband circuit, a 10 Mbps LTE backup, and a VSAT satellite link as a last resort. The satellite link had 700ms round-trip latency (geostationary orbit physics — the signal travels 72,000 km round trip) and 4-6% packet loss on a good day. The Versa SD-WAN appliance treated all three circuits as viable overlay paths. The default traffic steering policy measured latency, jitter, and loss and shifted traffic to the 'best' path. The problem: the satellite link was always the worst path, but the policy thresholds were set for terrestrial circuits. The satellite's metrics would oscillate around the threshold boundaries, causing the steering engine to continuously re-evaluate and shift traffic back and forth. VoIP calls were being moved to the satellite path for 10 seconds, then back to broadband, then back again — each shift causing an audible glitch. The fix was creating an explicit steering policy that excluded the satellite circuit from voice and real-time traffic classes entirely and reserved it only for best-effort data as a last-resort path. The lesson for any SD-WAN deployment: the out-of-box traffic steering policies are designed for relatively homogeneous WAN links. When you introduce a link with fundamentally different characteristics (satellite, highly congested LTE, circuits with asymmetric bandwidth), you need custom policies or you get oscillation.

lesson_pages.title: Troubleshooting Tunnels and ISP Circuit Issues
lesson_pages.body: During the 90-day POC, the Ferguson team built familiarity with Versa's troubleshooting workflow. Most issues fell into two categories: overlay tunnel failures and ISP circuit degradation. For tunnel failures, the Director's dashboard shows tunnel state, but the real troubleshooting happens on the appliance CLI. 'show interfaces tunnel' gives you the overlay tunnel status, including the underlay binding, encryption state, and traffic counters. If a tunnel shows down, the first check is whether the underlay circuit has a valid IP and default route. SD-WAN can't build an overlay if the underlay is broken — this sounds obvious but accounts for 40% of 'SD-WAN is down' tickets, which are really 'the ISP circuit is down' tickets. For ISP circuit degradation — the more subtle problem — Versa's per-path SLA monitoring continuously measures latency, jitter, and loss. The Analytics dashboard can show historical path quality and correlate application performance drops with circuit degradation events. This data is invaluable for ISP escalations: instead of saying 'our branch is slow,' you can show the ISP that their circuit experienced 8% packet loss between 2:00 and 3:30 PM with specific timestamp evidence. The POC demonstrated that SD-WAN doesn't eliminate ISP problems — Ferguson's branches still depend on whatever circuits are available in each market. What it does is make those problems visible and provide automatic failover when they occur.

headline: An Auditor Found an Undocumented Firewall Rule at a Ferguson DC. It Had Been There for 14 Months. The Change Ticket Was Never Created.
subheadline: The rule was technically correct. The access was authorized. But without a change record, it was a SOX finding — and it triggered a review of every firewall change made that fiscal year.

lesson_pages.title: The Audit Finding That Changed How Ferguson Does Change Management
lesson_pages.body: SOX Section 404 requires that publicly traded companies (Ferguson Enterprises is listed on the NYSE) maintain internal controls over financial reporting. The IT General Controls (ITGCs) are the subset that applies to technology systems — specifically, the systems that process, store, or transmit financial data. For a network engineer, this means every change to a device that handles financial transaction traffic is in scope. During a routine annual audit, the external audit firm identified a firewall rule permitting HTTPS access from a developer subnet to a financial reporting server. The rule was correctly configured — the access was legitimate and had been verbally approved by the application owner. But there was no change ticket. No approval record. No evidence of review. In SOX terms, this is a control deficiency. The control in question: 'All changes to in-scope systems must be documented, approved, tested, and reviewed before implementation.' The rule had been added during an emergency at 11 PM by a senior engineer who planned to create the ticket 'tomorrow.' Tomorrow never came. Fourteen months later, an auditor found it during a sample-based review of firewall rulesets. The remediation took three months: retroactive documentation, interviews with everyone involved, a formal gap assessment, and a commitment to additional monitoring controls. All for a rule that was technically correct.

lesson_pages.title: The Four ITGC Domains — What Auditors Actually Test
lesson_pages.body: SOX ITGCs cover four domains, and network engineers at Ferguson interact with all of them daily. Change Management: every network change must have a ticket, an approval (typically from a CAB or change manager), evidence of testing, and a post-implementation review. At Ferguson, this means no firewall rule, no ACL modification, no routing change goes in without a ServiceNow change request in 'Approved' status. The auditors sample 25-45 changes per audit cycle and verify end-to-end documentation. Access Control: who has access to in-scope systems and is that access appropriate? For network engineers, this means your access to routers, switches, and firewalls is reviewed quarterly. If you changed roles or left the team, your access must be removed within a defined timeframe (Ferguson's policy is 24 hours for terminations). Privileged access (enable mode, admin accounts) gets extra scrutiny. Computer Operations: are batch jobs running correctly? Are backups completing? Are monitoring alerts being responded to? For the network team, this means SNMP traps and syslog events for in-scope devices must be collected and retained. Program Development: when new systems are deployed, is there a documented SDLC process? For network, this applies to new site deployments and major infrastructure changes. The key insight: auditors aren't testing whether your network works. They're testing whether you can prove it works the way you say it does.

lesson_pages.title: Daily Life Under SOX — What Ferguson Network Engineers Actually Do
lesson_pages.body: SOX compliance at Ferguson isn't a once-a-year event — it's embedded in daily operations. Every Monday, the network team reviews the open change queue. Each change request includes: a description of the change, a business justification, a risk assessment (low/medium/high), a test plan, a rollback plan, an implementation window, and an approver. For standard changes (well-understood, low-risk, like adding a VLAN to an existing trunk), Ferguson uses pre-approved templates that skip CAB review. For normal changes (anything that modifies routing, firewall policy, or WAN configurations), CAB approval is required. For emergency changes, the engineer implements first and documents retroactively within 24 hours — but emergency changes get extra scrutiny during audits, so the team is incentivized to minimize them. Access reviews happen quarterly. The network team's manager receives a list of all personnel with access to in-scope network devices and must certify that each person still requires that access for their current role. Anyone who transferred out of the team must have their credentials revoked. This is verified by comparing the access list to HR records. The auditors will test this by selecting random terminated employees and checking whether their network access was removed within the policy window. Segregation of duties matters too — the person who requests a change should not be the same person who approves it. At Ferguson, network engineers submit changes, but the change manager (a separate role) approves them. This prevents a single individual from making unauthorized changes without oversight.

headline: A Single Trunk Port Left in PortFast Mode Caused a Broadcast Storm That Took Down 12 Ferguson Distribution Centers. STP Did Exactly What It Was Configured to Do — Which Was the Problem.
subheadline: The loop was in the topology from the moment the cable was plugged in. Spanning Tree was supposed to block it. Instead, a misconfigured exception meant 40,000 frames per second were circling the same four switches until the CPUs hit 100% and management plane access disappeared.

lesson_pages.title: What Spanning Tree Actually Prevents — and How It Fails
lesson_pages.body: Ethernet frames have no TTL. Unlike IP packets, which are dropped when the TTL field reaches zero, an Ethernet frame will circulate forever if there is a physical loop in the network. In a loop, a single broadcast frame — an ARP request, a DHCP discover, anything destined to FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF — is forwarded out every port except the one it arrived on. Two switches in a loop each forward the frame back to the other, which forwards it again, which forwards it again. Within milliseconds you have hundreds of thousands of frames per second consuming every available CPU cycle and link bandwidth. Every device on the segment is flooded. Management plane access disappears. The only recovery is to physically break the loop. Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) prevents this by electing a root bridge and then blocking redundant paths — putting certain ports into a Blocking state so that the logical topology has no loops, even if the physical topology does. The failure mode at Ferguson was not that STP was absent. It was that STP was bypassed on the exact port where the loop formed. A junior technician replacing a switch in a distribution center closet configured PortFast on a trunk port — something Cisco's documentation explicitly warns against. PortFast is designed for access ports connected to end stations. It skips the Listening and Learning states (normally 30 seconds total) and immediately places the port into Forwarding. This is correct and useful for workstation ports where you want DHCP and authentication to proceed quickly. On a trunk port connected to another switch, PortFast means that when the cable is plugged in, the port forwards immediately — before STP has had a chance to detect that a loop now exists. The loop was there the instant the uplink was connected.

lesson_pages.title: Root Bridge Election and Port Roles — What STP Computes
lesson_pages.body: STP begins by electing a root bridge — the switch that becomes the center of the spanning tree. Every other switch computes the lowest-cost path to the root and assigns port roles accordingly. The root bridge is elected based on bridge ID, which is a combination of a configurable priority value (default 32768) and the switch MAC address. The switch with the lowest bridge ID wins. At Ferguson, the distribution layer switches are manually configured with lower priority values (4096 or 8192) to ensure they win the root bridge election, not an access layer switch with default priority. This matters because the root bridge placement determines which ports end up blocked. If an access switch accidentally becomes root — as happens when a new switch with default priority is connected to a network where all existing switches also have default priority — the spanning tree recalculates with the new root, ports that were previously in Forwarding may go to Blocking, and ports that were Blocking may go to Forwarding. During recalculation, which takes 30–50 seconds in classic STP (less in Rapid STP), some paths are temporarily unavailable. Port roles: Root Port — the port on each non-root switch with the lowest cost path to the root bridge. Designated Port — the port on each segment that is closest to the root bridge (forwards traffic for that segment). Non-Designated Port (Alternate/Backup in RSTP) — the blocked port that prevents the loop. If the root port fails, the blocked port transitions to Forwarding after reconvergence. Knowing which ports STP will block is essential for designing networks where the redundant path, when it becomes active, does not cause routing asymmetry or unexpected traffic patterns.

lesson_pages.title: Rapid STP, BPDU Guard, and the Ferguson Branch Design
lesson_pages.body: Classic STP (802.1D) takes 30–50 seconds to converge after a topology change. In a retail environment where POS transactions time out after 30 seconds, this is unacceptable. Ferguson runs Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP, 802.1w) on all switches, which achieves convergence in under a second for most topology changes by using a proposal/agreement handshake between connected switches instead of waiting out fixed timers. RSTP also introduces explicit port roles (Alternate and Backup) and edge ports (equivalent to PortFast) to distinguish between ports connected to end stations versus ports connected to other switches. The critical protection mechanism at Ferguson branches is BPDU Guard on all access ports. A BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Unit) is the STP control message that switches exchange to elect the root bridge and calculate the spanning tree. End stations — workstations, POS terminals, printers — should never send BPDUs. If a BPDU Guard-enabled access port receives a BPDU, it immediately shuts itself down (err-disabled state). This prevents an unauthorized switch from being plugged into an access port and potentially influencing the spanning tree topology or creating a loop. The incident that took down the distribution centers was caused by a trunk port configured with PortFast — the edge port designation — which means BPDU Guard was also active on that port. When the new switch was connected and began sending BPDUs, BPDU Guard should have err-disabled the port. But due to a switch software bug on a specific firmware version, BPDU Guard failed to trigger on trunk ports configured with PortFast. The loop persisted for 4 minutes before the technician physically disconnected the cable.

lesson_pages.title: Diagnosing STP Issues at Ferguson Scale
lesson_pages.body: After the distribution center incident, Ferguson's network team built a systematic STP health check process. The commands to know: 'show spanning-tree' on the root bridge candidates shows the current root, bridge priority, and port states across all VLANs. 'show spanning-tree inconsistentports' identifies ports stuck in BPDUGuard-inconsistent or RootGuard-inconsistent states, which usually indicates a topology problem. 'show spanning-tree detail' shows topology change counts — a high and rising topology change counter on a stable network indicates a flapping link somewhere, which forces STP to repeatedly age out MAC address tables and flood traffic. 'show errdisable recovery' shows any ports that have been shut down by BPDU Guard and whether auto-recovery is configured. At Ferguson's scale — 1,700 branches, each with 1–3 access switches — individual switch monitoring is handled by the NMS. The STP-specific monitoring rule: any port that transitions to err-disabled state generates an alert within 60 seconds. Any root bridge change on a distribution VLAN generates a P1 alert immediately, because an unexpected root bridge change at the distribution layer has network-wide impact. The post-incident fix was a global configuration policy: PortFast is never configured manually. Instead, the switch templates use 'spanning-tree portfast default' (which applies PortFast only to access ports, not trunks) combined with 'spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default' at the global level. Individual port overrides require a change ticket with explicit justification. Root Guard is applied on all downlink ports from distribution to access switches, preventing an access switch from ever claiming the root bridge role even if its priority is manipulated.

headline: A PCI Auditor Asked: 'Show Me Your Network Diagram for the Cardholder Data Environment.' Ferguson's Was 18 Months Out of Date. That's a Finding Before You Even Start.
subheadline: PCI DSS Requirement 1.1.2 mandates a current network diagram showing all connections to cardholder data. 'Current' means current — not 'last year's Visio with some handwritten notes.'

lesson_pages.title: The Branch POS Environment — 1,700 CDEs and Counting
lesson_pages.body: Every Ferguson branch has at least one point-of-sale terminal that processes credit card transactions. That terminal, the network segment it sits on, and every device that touches that traffic path — from the branch switch port to the payment processor — is part of the Cardholder Data Environment (CDE). PCI DSS applies to all of it. With 1,700 branches, Ferguson effectively has 1,700 micro-CDEs that must be individually segmented, monitored, and validated. Scope reduction through network segmentation is the single most important architectural decision. If the POS terminal sits on the same VLAN as the branch workstations and printers, then every device on that VLAN is in scope for PCI. Every workstation needs hardening. Every printer needs vulnerability scanning. Every switch port needs monitoring. This is operationally impossible at scale. Instead, Ferguson places POS terminals on a dedicated VLAN with ACLs restricting traffic to only the payment processor endpoints. The switch enforces segmentation. The firewall validates it. If the segmentation is validated — typically by an annual penetration test that attempts to cross from the corporate VLAN to the POS VLAN — then only the POS VLAN, the switch ports connected to it, and the uplink path are in scope. This reduces the PCI scope from 'the entire branch network' to 'one VLAN and a few ACLs.' That's the difference between a manageable compliance program and an impossible one.

lesson_pages.title: Requirements 1 and 6 — What Network Engineers Own
lesson_pages.body: PCI DSS has 12 top-level requirements. Network engineers primarily own two of them. Requirement 1: Install and maintain network security controls (firewalls and segmentation). This includes maintaining a current network diagram, documenting all firewall and router rules that permit traffic into the CDE, implementing a formal process for reviewing and approving all network connections and firewall rule changes, and conducting firewall rule reviews every six months to remove stale or unnecessary rules. At Ferguson, the semi-annual firewall rule review is a significant effort. Each branch's ACLs and the data center firewall rules that touch POS traffic must be examined. Rules that no longer serve a business purpose must be removed. Rules that are too broad must be tightened. The review must be documented and signed off. Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications. For network engineers, this means keeping firmware current — applying security patches to switches, routers, and firewalls within a defined timeframe after release. It also means following change control procedures for any modification to in-scope devices. You cannot push a firmware update to a switch that carries POS traffic without a change ticket, a test plan, and an approval. Every PCI audit at Ferguson starts with the QSA (Qualified Security Assessor) asking for the network diagram, the firewall rule review documentation, and the change log for in-scope devices. If any of these are missing or outdated, you're starting the audit with findings already on the board.

lesson_pages.title: Wireless Security and the Branch Perimeter
lesson_pages.body: PCI DSS has specific requirements for wireless networks in or near the CDE. If a Ferguson branch has a wireless access point, the QSA will ask: is the wireless network segmented from the POS VLAN? Is WPA2/WPA3 Enterprise with 802.1X authentication enforced? Are rogue access point scans performed quarterly? Can a contractor standing in the parking lot with a laptop reach the POS network? Ferguson's branch wireless design places Wi-Fi on a completely separate VLAN from POS, with no routing between them. The wireless VLAN provides guest internet access and connectivity for mobile handheld devices used for inventory management. The inventory handhelds don't process payment data, so they're out of PCI scope — but only if the network segmentation is enforced and validated. The quarterly rogue AP detection is operationally significant at 1,700 locations. Ferguson uses a combination of wireless IDS built into the access points and periodic sweeps using a managed security service. A rogue AP — an unauthorized wireless access point plugged into the network — could bridge an attacker from the parking lot directly onto the wired network. If that rogue AP is plugged into the POS VLAN, it's a PCI breach waiting to happen. Port security (limiting MAC addresses per switch port) and 802.1X wired authentication are the controls that prevent a rogue AP from functioning even if someone physically plugs one in.

lesson_pages.title: What Auditors Ask For — The PCI Evidence Checklist
lesson_pages.body: Over multiple PCI audit cycles, Ferguson's network team has learned exactly what the QSA will request. Having these ready before the audit starts is the difference between a smooth assessment and a fire drill. The checklist: current network diagrams showing all CDE connections, data flows, and segmentation points (Requirement 1.1.2). Firewall and ACL rulesets for all in-scope devices, with business justification for each rule (Requirement 1.1.1). Evidence of the semi-annual firewall rule review — minutes, attendees, findings, remediation actions (Requirement 1.1.7). Change tickets for any in-scope device modifications since the last assessment — the QSA will sample 10-15 and verify each has proper approval, testing, and documentation (Requirement 6.4). Firmware versions for all in-scope network devices, with evidence that known vulnerabilities have been patched or mitigated within 30 days of patch availability (Requirement 6.3.3). Wireless scan results showing no rogue APs were detected — or if detected, evidence of remediation (Requirement 11.2.1). Penetration test results validating that segmentation controls are effective — specifically, that the tester could not cross from the corporate network to the CDE (Requirement 11.4.5). For a network engineer at Ferguson, PCI compliance is not something you do once a year. It's a continuous operational discipline: every firewall change, every firmware update, every new branch deployment must be done with PCI in mind because the evidence of how you operate daily is exactly what the auditor examines annually.
